Article.ArticleTitle: Estimation of seasonal ecological water demand in arid zone of Northwest China: An approach using the LSTM-random forest regression model.

Abstract.AbstractText: As a typical ecologically fragile area in the arid zone of Northwest China, the contradiction between water supply and demand has become increasingly prominent. A dynamic assessment of watershed ecological water demand is imperative for the sustainable development of regional water resources. A substantial body of research was previously conducted on the ecological water demand assessment in arid regions. However, these studies predominantly relied on deterministic model frameworks. The single output results generated by such frameworks were deficient in their inability to adequately characterize the uncertainties induced by climate change and anthropogenic factors, potentially leading to deviations from expected outcomes. To address the shortcomings of the previously used methods, this study constructed a coupled LSTM-Random Forest Regression model based on Bayesian optimization. The probability modification coefficient (PMC) was introduced to achieve an interval-range prediction that overcome the shortcomings of single-value prediction from the conventional approach. This improvement largely mitigated the issue where future predicted values of the model deviate significantly from actual values due to uncertain factors. Concurrently, the Sen-Mann-Kendall (Sen-MK) trend test method was employed to conduct an in-depth analysis of the indicative significance of the spatiotemporal evolution pattern of fractional vegetation cover (FVC) for ecological water demand, providing a reference for setting future FVC thresholds. The proposed approach was applied to the Shiyang River Basin (SRB) in Northwest China. Our study demonstrated that the coupled model attained a highly precise prediction of FVC (test set R2 = 0.951, MSE = 0.597). The estimation of the ecological water demand of the lower SRB in 2022, revealed a significant seasonal divergence pattern. The summer months (May-September) required a significant increase in the amount of water coming from upstream in the lower part of the basin, while late spring (March-April) was characterized by low water consumption and high ecological benefits. In the future, priority should be given to ensuring early spring water replenishment and summer buffer water supply in the lower reaches of the SRB. The proposed coupled model in this study provided a technical framework that was able to resolve the uncertainties in the assessment of dynamic ecological water demand in arid zones. Outcomes of this study can provide a scientific basis for a better water resources management strategy in arid zones.
